original_abstract: The rapid expansion of machine learning (ML) across diverse scientific and industrial domains has created a critical need for rigorous performance evaluation and comparative analysis. However, selecting appropriate metrics and applying the correct statistical frameworks remains a challenge for many researchers. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of essential evaluation metrics tailored to various supervised learning paradigms, including binary, multi-class, and multi-label classification, regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. Beyond simple metric calculation, we address the vital role of statistical hypothesis testing in determining whether observed performance differences between models are significant or merely the result of stochastic variation. We discuss the limitations of commonly misused tests, such as the paired t-test, and advocate for robust non-parametric alternatives like the Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman, McNemar, and DeLong tests, particularly when dealing with resampled data or non-normal distributions. To demonstrate these principles in practice, we present experimental case studies utilizing convolutional neural networks for medical imaging tasks, specifically the classification of lung infections from X-rays and the segmentation of cancerous tumors in PET images. This work serves as a practical guide for ensuring methodological integrity in the assessment and comparison of machine learning models.
humanized_abstract: In these exciting times of machine learning (ML) being applied to a wide array of scientific and industrial applications, there is a pressing need for validating and comparing the performance of ML approaches. Choosing the right performance measures and applying the right statistical tools to assess their performance is not straightforward for many researchers. In this review paper, we provide an overview of the most commonly used performance measures appropriate to the different supervised learning setups: - Binary, multi-class and multi-label classification - Regression - Image segmentation - Object detection - Information retrieval We also highlight the importance of statistical hypothesis testing in ascertaining whether the differences in performance estimates of the models being compared are statistically significant or not. We caution the misuse of the paired t-test and advocate for the use of non-parametric tests such as the Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman, McNemar and DeLong tests, especially when validating performances on resampled datasets or data samples with non-Gaussian distributions. To illustrate the usefulness of the above performance measures and statistical tests, we present several experimental examples of applications of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to medical imaging where the classification of lung infections from X-ray images and the segmentation of different types of cancers from positron emission tomography (PET) images are considered. This review aims to serve as a reference guide in assessing the validity of different ML approaches.
